$schema-note: Fixture corpus for ilb-llm-fix v1.3. Each fixture pairs a lint finding with buggy code derived from a real pattern in ~/repos/ofriperetz.dev/oos/, a known-good fix, and a verifier the model output must satisfy. The `source` block cites the oos/ repo + file the pattern was extracted or adapted from. See ./corpus.md for the full citation table. v1.3 adds: hardcoded-jwt-secret (nestjs), eval-on-dynamic-import (payload), foreach-async-floating-promise (strapi), insecure-random-api-key (cal.com). Corpus now 12 fixtures (was 8 in v1.2).

source.note: Real-world NestJS JWT guard sample. The official sample passes only `{ secret }` with no `algorithms` whitelist, leaving the verifier vulnerable to alg-confusion attacks if the secret is ever interpretable as a public key.

v1.description: JWT verify call lacks an explicit algorithms whitelist
v1.fix: Pin the allowed algorithms, e.g. { algorithms: ['HS256'] }

v2.description: JWT verify call lacks an explicit algorithms whitelist
v2.why: Without an algorithms whitelist, attackers can downgrade or confuse the verification (alg=none, RS256→HS256 confusion using the public key as the HMAC secret).
v2.fix: Pin the allowed algorithms, e.g. { algorithms: ['HS256'] }

verify.reason: Fix must add an algorithms whitelist option to the verify call

source.note: Three.js examples index uses `div.innerHTML = htmlString.trim()` to inject filter results. Safe in this case (htmlString is internally constructed), but a fixture where htmlString comes from user input is a real CWE-79 pattern.

v1.description: Untrusted input written to innerHTML enables XSS
v1.fix: Use textContent, or sanitize with DOMPurify before assignment

v2.description: Untrusted input written to innerHTML enables XSS
v2.why: innerHTML parses script tags and on* event handlers from the input string, so user-controlled data becomes executable code.
v2.fix: Use textContent for plain strings, or sanitize with DOMPurify when HTML is required

verify.reason: Fix must stop assigning userQuery to innerHTML and use textContent or a sanitizer

source.note: shadcn-ui uses `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` legitimately to inject a static theme-detection script before hydration. Adapted here to a hypothetical user-input variant — the lint rule should still flag the unsafe pattern.

v1.description: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML used on untrusted input
v1.fix: Render as text, or sanitize the HTML with DOMPurify first

v2.description: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML used on untrusted input
v2.why: dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses React's automatic escaping. User-controlled HTML becomes executable script in the rendered DOM.
v2.fix: Render the value as a text child instead, or run it through DOMPurify.sanitize before injection

verify.reason: Fix must not pass userBio through dangerouslySetInnerHTML — use a text child or sanitize first

source.note: Medusa's admin Vite plugin uses MD5 for non-cryptographic content fingerprinting (cache key generation). This is a known FP pattern for naive `no-md5` rules. Acceptable fix should either switch to SHA-256 or, ideally, document why MD5 is fine here. We accept either — see verifier.

v1.description: MD5 is broken for cryptographic use
v1.fix: Use SHA-256 or annotate as non-cryptographic content fingerprint only

v2.description: MD5 hash detected — broken for cryptographic use
v2.why: MD5 collisions are trivially generatable; must not be used for integrity, authentication, or signatures. Non-cryptographic content fingerprints (e.g. cache keys) are an acceptable use, but should be explicitly marked so the linter and future readers know it's intentional.
v2.fix: Switch to SHA-256, OR keep MD5 with a comment explicitly stating it's a non-cryptographic content fingerprint

verify.reason: Two acceptable fix paths: switch off MD5 (SHA-256 / stronger) OR keep MD5 with an explicit non-cryptographic comment. Either passes.

options.reason: Fix moved off MD5 to a stronger algorithm
Fix kept MD5 but added an explicit non-cryptographic-use comment

source.repo: (generic — pattern present across payload, medusa, strapi, twentyhq)
source.note: Circular import between two domain modules sharing a type. The most common shape across the oos/ TS server codebases is: A imports concrete from B, B imports type from A → cycle.

v1.description: Circular import detected: src/A.ts → src/B.ts → src/A.ts
v1.fix: Extract shared types to src/types.ts and import from there

v2.description: Circular import detected: src/A.ts → src/B.ts → src/A.ts
v2.why: Circular imports cause undefined values at module init time and create unpredictable load order. Extracting the shared type breaks the cycle without changing runtime behavior.
v2.fix: Extract shared types to src/types.ts and import from there

verify.reason: Fix must break the A→B→A cycle (likely by extracting shared types)

source.repo: (generic — pattern common in payload, medusa, strapi, twentyhq backends)
source.note: Iterating a collection and awaiting a per-row DB lookup is a textbook N+1. Common shape across the oos/ Node ORM-backed services.

v1.description: N+1 query: per-row DB fetch inside an async loop
v1.fix: Replace the per-row fetch with a single batched query (IN clause or .where({ id: { in: ids } }))

v2.description: N+1 query: per-row DB fetch inside an async loop
v2.why: Each iteration triggers a network round-trip to the database, so latency scales linearly with collection size. A single batched query collapses the work to one round-trip.
v2.fix: Replace the per-row fetch with a single batched query (e.g. `IN` clause or `findMany({ where: { id: { in: ids } } })`)
v2.vulnerable: for (const row of rows) { row.detail = await db.findOne(row.id); }
v2.safe: const details = await db.findMany({ where: { id: { in: rows.map(r => r.id) } } });

verify.reason: Fix must remove the per-row await db.profile.findUnique inside the for loop

source.note: Cal.com's Apple Calendar credential check uses `decryptedKey.password === password` (line 82) — a textbook CWE-208 timing-attack vector. An attacker can guess the password byte-by-byte by measuring response time of the early-exit `===` comparison.

v1.description: Secret comparison uses === (early-exit) — vulnerable to timing attacks
v1.fix: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on Buffers of equal length

v2.description: Secret comparison uses === (early-exit) — vulnerable to timing attacks
v2.why: JavaScript's === short-circuits on first character mismatch. An attacker can measure response time across many guesses to recover the secret one character at a time. Use a constant-time comparison.
v2.fix: Replace === with crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(a), Buffer.from(b)) — both inputs must be the same byte length, so check length separately first.
v2.safe: const a = Buffer.from(decryptedKey.password); const b = Buffer.from(password); if (a.length === b.length && crypto.timingSafeEqual(a, b)) { ... }

verify.reason: Fix must replace === comparison with crypto.timingSafeEqual

source.note: NestJS's official JWT auth sample exports `jwtConstants.secret` as a hardcoded string. The string itself self-deprecates ('DO NOT USE THIS VALUE...') but the pattern is the textbook CWE-798 anti-pattern that any project copying the sample inherits.

v1.description: JWT signing secret hardcoded in source
v1.fix: Read the secret from process.env at module init

v2.description: JWT signing secret hardcoded in source
v2.why: Secrets in source code leak through git history, CI logs, container images, and crash dumps. They cannot be rotated without a code deploy. JWT signing secrets in particular grant token-forging capability.
v2.fix: Read the secret from `process.env.JWT_SECRET` at module init; throw if missing.
v2.safe: if (!process.env.JWT_SECRET) throw new Error('JWT_SECRET env var required'); export const jwtConstants = { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET };

verify.reason: Fix must read secret from process.env and remove the hardcoded string literal

source.note: Payload's dynamicImport.ts wraps a string-interpolated import path in eval() to dodge Next.js bundler static analysis. The comment is explicit about the workaround. While the input here happens to be developer-controlled, the same pattern in any user-input-touching code path is a CWE-95 vector.

v1.description: eval() executed on a string built via template interpolation
v1.fix: Replace with native dynamic import() — works in modern bundlers and avoids eval entirely

v2.description: eval() executed on a string built via template interpolation
v2.why: Wrapping import() in eval() bypasses bundler analysis but also opens a CWE-95 vector if the interpolated value ever comes from user input. Native dynamic import() is now supported in all modern bundlers — no eval needed.

verify.reason: Fix must remove the eval() call and use native dynamic import()

source.repo: (generic — pattern present in strapi tests, medusa, twentyhq)
source.note: Array.forEach(async (...) => { await ... }) returns void from each iteration's async callback. Promises float, errors are silently swallowed, and the for-loop returns before any awaited work completes. Common shape across the oos/ TS codebases — the strapi instance is in a test file but the anti-pattern shape is what matters.

v1.description: Array.forEach with async callback creates floating promises
v1.fix: Use a for...of loop or Promise.all(array.map(async ...))

v2.description: Array.forEach with async callback creates floating promises
v2.why: forEach ignores return values, so async callbacks return discarded Promises. The outer function continues before any iteration's await resolves, and any rejection becomes an unhandled promise rejection — silent data corruption in production.
v2.fix: Use `for (const x of arr) { await fn(x); }` for sequential, or `await Promise.all(arr.map(fn))` for parallel.

verify.reason: Fix must remove the forEach(async (...) =>) anti-pattern — use for-of or Promise.all(map)

source.note: Cal.com's Make integration setup builds an API key string with `Math.random().toString(36).substring(2)` (line 38). Math.random() is a non-cryptographic PRNG — the resulting key is predictable to an attacker who can observe a few samples and recover the seed.

v1.description: Math.random() used to generate a security token

v2.description: Math.random() used to generate a security token
v2.why: Math.random() is a non-cryptographic PRNG — Mersenne Twister or similar. Its output is predictable: an attacker who observes a handful of generated values can recover the internal state and predict every future output. Never use it for tokens, session IDs, or secrets.
v2.fix: Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` for opaque tokens, or `crypto.randomUUID()` for UUIDs.

verify.reason: Fix must replace Math.random() with a CSPRNG (crypto.randomBytes / randomUUID / getRandomValues)

source.note: Payload's main package barrel re-exports from across the codebase (1893 lines as of 2026-05-03). Importing from the barrel for a single helper drags in the entire transitive dep graph — defeats tree-shaking and slows cold-start.

v1.description: Import from a deep barrel file pulls in unrelated transitive deps
v1.fix: Import directly from the source module instead of through the barrel

v2.description: Import from a deep barrel file pulls in unrelated transitive deps
v2.why: Bundlers retain every transitive member of a barrel even when only one is used (poor tree-shaking on re-export chains). For a 1900-line barrel, this can mean hundreds of KB of dead code in the final bundle and seconds added to cold-start.
v2.fix: Import the specific helper from its source module, e.g. `import { sanitizeConfig } from 'payload/dist/config/sanitize'` instead of `import { sanitizeConfig } from 'payload'`.

verify.reason: Fix must import from the deep path (e.g. 'payload/dist/config/sanitize'), not the top-level 'payload' barrel
